questions.prompt: Your agent averages $0.04/run. Total bill is 3× expected. Average looks fine. Where's the cost actually hiding?

choices.text: In the average — you misread it.

In the tail — typically p99 runs are 10–100× the median. A 1% tail at $5/run can dominate a 99% body at $0.03/run. You need p50/p95/p99 metrics, not just average. Investigate every run above a threshold (e.g. $1+) until you understand what's driving the tail.

questions.explanation: Averages mislead on agent costs because the distribution is heavy-tailed. The 1% tail can be 50%+ of your bill. Track p50/p95/p99, alert on tail growth (not just averages), investigate expensive runs until patterns emerge. This is where production cost work actually lives.

questions.prompt: Which is the most common cause of silent agent cost regressions?

choices.text: A prompt change introducing variance into a previously stable prefix — killing the prompt cache. Per-call cost on the cached portion jumps from ~10% rate to 100% rate. The change looks innocent in a code review but daily cost can quadruple silently. Drift alerts on cache hit rate / per-call cost catch this in hours instead of weeks.

questions.explanation: Cache regressions are the most common silent cost regression. A timestamp, request ID, or user-specific data templated into 'stable' content invalidates the cache and per-call cost jumps. Drift alerts on cache hit rate and per-call cost catch the regression while the bill is still small.
